reviews.feedback: The output contradicts the skill's own philosophy. The skill says "the only way to know is to measure" but this output produces "Expected Results" with predicted percentages (20-45%) instead of actual benchmark numbers. The without_skill version actually ran the benchmarks and reported real timings (465µs → 218µs). The output is also not self-contained — it's 3 loose files that require manual Cargo.toml setup to run. The report recommends preallocated based on theory, while empirical data (from the without_skill run) shows sort_scan actually wins at production scale. This is exactly the kind of "reasoning from theory" the skill was designed to prevent. The skill should either require actually running the benchmark, or at minimum not produce a "recommendation" section until real data exists.

Excellent output. Produces a complete, runnable Cargo project with actual benchmark results (465µs → 218µs, 2.14x speedup). The recommendation is backed by measured data, not predictions. Includes a scaling table showing behavior at 500/2000/10000 pagos. The recommended implementation is provided as copy-paste-ready code. The analysis correctly identifies that sort_scan wins at production scale while presized edges ahead at 10k — nuanced, data-driven advice. The only minor issue: the recommended implementation uses &Pago references (sorted Vec of references) which requires the input slice to outlive the sort, but this is fine for the use case. This is what the with_skill version should have produced.

Good analysis structure but same fundamental problem as eval-1: it predicts results ("expected 3-10x speedup") rather than measuring them. The crossover point analysis is theoretical ("At n≤15: negligible difference") without actual timing data. Recommends sort_partition but can't prove it's better because no benchmarks were actually run. The without_skill version measured real crossover at n=80-100 and made the opposite recommendation (keep the original) with concrete justification: the 218µs worst case is noise compared to DB I/O. The with_skill version's recommendation to optimize is arguably wrong — the without_skill version's "don't optimize" recommendation is better-reasoned because it considers the full system context (DB latency dominates). The skill's prescriptive workflow led to a worse decision here.

Outstanding. This is the best output across all 6 runs. It ran actual benchmarks, found the crossover point empirically (n=80-100), and made the contrarian but correct recommendation: don't optimize. The reasoning is excellent — it considers absolute times (218µs is nothing), the surrounding system context (DB I/O dominates), and the complexity cost of the optimization. The "When WOULD optimization be warranted?" section shows mature engineering judgment. This demonstrates that sometimes the right answer to "should we optimize?" is "no" — and backing that with data is more valuable than blindly optimizing. The skill should learn from this: a good benchmark report can recommend NOT changing the code.

Same pattern: theoretical predictions ("~200x reduction in work", "~50-100μs build cost") without actual measurements. The without_skill version measured 30x speedup (not 200x) and 1ms build cost (not 50-100μs) — the predictions were off by 6x and 10-20x respectively. This demonstrates exactly why measuring matters: theoretical analysis predicted 200x but reality delivered 30x. The report structure is good (tradeoff table, implementation plan, amortization discussion) but the numbers are fiction. The recommendation happens to be correct (adopt the index) but for overstated reasons. If the skill's purpose is "measure, don't guess," it's failing at its own mission.

Strong output with real benchmark data. Reports 30x speedup for the dominant query pattern with concrete timings (39.2µs → 1.29µs). The scaling table clearly shows O(n) vs O(1) behavior. Index build cost is honestly reported at 1ms (not the 50-100µs the with_skill version predicted). The fallback behavior is tested and shown to be no worse (1.2x faster actually). The implementation uses Vec<usize> indices rather than Vec<&Pago> references, which is a better design choice (avoids lifetime complexity, smaller memory footprint). Minor note: the 1ms build cost is higher than expected for 5000 items — might be worth investigating if the HashMap is being built inefficiently, but it's still negligible for the use case.
